student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Reactivating Your Professional Voice -- Roles, Responsibilities and Ongoing Projects
lesson.menu_desc: Describing what you own at Motiva and running a project retrospective with the third conditional -- 37 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 2 -- Reactivating Your Professional Voice
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Aula 2 -- Reactivating Your Professional Voice

listenings.text: Hello João, Elena Vidal here, as promised. I looked into the interlocking with our procurement team this morning. The lead time is confirmed at nine months, so the equipment lands in São Paulo in January, not in October. That is the bad news. The good news is that our subcontractor can start the cabling as soon as the interface document is signed, which means the field work does not have to wait for the hardware. One of the main challenges I see is the handover date, because it still assumes an October delivery. Could you send me the updated schedule for the handover before Wednesday? I would rather bring a realistic date to the steering committee than a date nobody believes. Thank you, and talk to you on Wednesday.

Good morning, everyone. This is a lessons-learned meeting, not a court, so I want to be very clear from the start. I am not interested in blaming the subcontractor. The problem was our process, not one company. Let us look at the facts. If procurement had been involved in the design review, the order would have been placed in January and the lead time would not have hurt the schedule. If the interface document had been signed before the order, we would have avoided four months of rework. If somebody had looked into the first alarm in March, that alarm would not have become a serious problem in June. And honestly, if we had been open about the delay earlier, the client would have accepted a new date without a fight. So here is the one rule I want for the next package: the interface document is signed before any order is placed. No exceptions. Everything else on my list is negotiable, but that one is not.

reading.rtitle: Project Brief -- CBTC Signaling Upgrade, Line 5 Extension (internal, for new suppliers)
paras: Motiva is the main contractor for the CBTC signaling upgrade on the Line 5 extension. The signaling workstream is owned by João Guilherme da Costa e Silva, who specializes in CBTC and coordinates both the design team and the field team. He liaises with all the suppliers on the package and carries out the factory inspections in Europe. His counterpart at each supplier signs the interface document that defines how the two systems connect.

The project is currently in the process of closing the design review. The review should have been closed in January, but the interface document from the previous supplier arrived three months late. If that document had arrived on time, the design review would have been completed before the summer and the field installation would already be under way.

The document, however, is only the tip of the iceberg. The lead time for the interlocking is nine months, and procurement placed the order in April. The cabling is carried out by a local subcontractor, who cannot start until the interface document is signed. The handover to the operator is still officially planned for June, and nobody on the team believes that date. The new supplier is asked to look into the interlocking delivery and to come up with a realistic schedule before the next steering committee.

reading.text: Notice the collocations: carry out an inspection, liaise with a supplier, look into a delay, come up with a schedule, sign an interface document.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the brief?
choices: Motiva is looking for a new subcontractor to do the cabling on Line 5.
A new supplier is briefed on who owns the signaling workstream and why the project is late.
The interlocking has a nine-month lead time and the order was placed in April.
The design review was closed in January, as planned.

items: João carries out the factory inspections himself.
"He liaises with all the suppliers on the package and carries out the factory inspections in Europe."
The design review was closed on time.
It should have been closed in January, but the interface document arrived three months late.
The subcontractor can start the cabling before the interface document is signed.
"...who cannot start until the interface document is signed."
The team believes the June handover date.
"...and nobody on the team believes that date."
The new supplier is asked to propose a realistic schedule.
"...to come up with a realistic schedule before the next steering committee."

items.situation: "So, what exactly do you own on this project?"
tips: I'm responsible for the signaling workstream on the Line 5 extension.
My role involves coordinating the design and field teams.

items.situation: "Who do you deal with on the supplier side?"
tips: I liaise with every supplier on the package.
My counterpart in Madrid signs the interface document.

items.situation: "Why is the design review still open?"
tips: If the interface document had arrived in January, we would have closed the review before the summer.

items.situation: "Is the late document the only problem?"
tips: Honestly, the document is only the tip of the iceberg.
The lead time for the interlocking is nine months.
If procurement had been involved earlier, the order would have been placed in January.
Describe the process, not the person.

items.situation: "I don't have that answer with me. What do you do?"
tips: I will look into it and come back to you on Friday.
Let me come up with a realistic date first.
